title: We only have 2 years...
summary: Matthew Berman discusses a two-year window for democracies to maintain AI leadership, citing competitive pressures across four fronts and referencing Anthropic research on 2028 AI leadership trajectories. The video examines the state of global AI competition and implications for ensuring democratic nations lead in AI development.
writeup: A point-by-point review of Anthropic's essay '2028: Two Scenarios for Global AI Leadership,' which argues that compute access (chips from Nvidia, Google TPUs, AWS Trainium) is the single most critical AI ingredient, more so than data or researchers. The essay frames the contest as a race between democracies and the CCP over who sets global AI norms, with 2028 as the inflection point. The reviewer endorses Anthropic's problem diagnosis, including the underappreciated role of export controls and China's distillation attacks, but diverges sharply on the proposed solutions, and adds his own hypothesis that 2028 likely marks the arrival of self-improving AI, which changes the calculus entirely.
key_points: Anthropic identifies compute (chips) as the most important ingredient for frontier AI development, ahead of data and researchers, and neither Anthropic nor OpenAI manufactures its own silicon, relying entirely on purchased or rented capacity from Nvidia, Google, and AWS.

China's AI labs have reached near-frontier capability through three mechanisms: world-class research talent (Jensen Huang has noted roughly 50% of AI researchers globally are Chinese), systematic exploitation of loopholes in US export controls including chip smuggling and proxy compute access, and large-scale distillation attacks that extract innovations from American models, though the scale of DeepSeek's distillation is contested.

Anthropic's two scenarios for 2028 are: democracies tighten export controls and accelerate AI adoption, holding their compute lead and setting global AI norms; or America fails to act, China reaches the frontier, and authoritarian regimes shape AI governance with automated repression at scale.

The reviewer's key addition to Anthropic's framing is that 2028 likely marks the emergence of self-improving AI, and whoever reaches that threshold first wins in a way that neither scenario in the essay fully accounts for.

The DeepSeek team is cited as evidence of China's genuine algorithmic talent: their ability to achieve significant efficiency unlocks under heavy compute constraints is described as 'beyond impressive,' independent of any distillation question.

The CCP's existing AI deployment includes mass surveillance, facial recognition infrastructure, speech censorship, dissident repression, and state-sponsored hacking of foreign governments and corporations, activities that predate the current frontier race and shaped China's early AI research priorities.
